children.value: In this chapter, we transition from initial sizing into the detailed aerodynamic characteristics of our aircraft. For any practical design—especially a 3D-printed UAV where battery capacity and motor size are tightly constrained—a reliable drag estimate is essential.
What is the total aircraft drag coefficient (

children.html: CDC_DCD​

children.value: ) for a given geometry and flight condition?
During the conceptual design phase, estimating drag accurately determines:
Overall feasibility and cost of the vehicle
Instead of running high-fidelity Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) on day one, we rely on a
method. This allows us to predict the aircraft

children.html: CDC_DCD​

children.value: based on empirical relationships applied to individual geometric components, making it perfect for rapid iterations in our sizing codes.

children.alt: A standard general aviation aircraft (such as the Cirrus SR22) used for drag build-up example calculations.

children.value: A standard general aviation aircraft (such as the Cirrus SR22) used for drag build-up example calculations.
Total drag is broadly categorized into three types:

children.html: CD,0C_{D,0}CD,0​
CD,pC_{D,p}CD,p​

children.value: Drag that is always present, regardless of lift. This is highly dependent on the wetted area and aerodynamic shape.

children.html: CD,iC_{D,i}CD,i​
CD,cC_{D,c}CD,c​
CD,wC_{D,w}CD,w​

children.value: Drag due to supersonic effects. For our 3D-printed UAVs, which operate at low subsonic speeds, this term is generally negligible.
Thus, our total drag coefficient is typically:

children.html: CD=CD,0+CD,iC_D = C_{D,0} + C_{D,i}CD​=CD,0​+CD,i​

children.value: Parasite drag, or “zero-lift” drag, is always present and is strongly tied to boundary layer behavior. It consists of three main components:
Due to air “scrubbing” the surface. It is dependent on whether the boundary layer is laminar or turbulent.
Due to the body pushing flow out of the way, resulting in a non-ideal pressure distribution. Thicker bodies generally have higher pressure drag.
Caused by flow from one component mixing and interfering with flow from another (e.g., wing-fuselage junctions).
To estimate the total subsonic parasite drag, we add up the contributions of all wetted components exposed to the flow and include corrections for miscellaneous items (like landing gear or gaps).
(C_{D,0}) \cdot S_{ref} = \sum_{i} \left( C_{f,i} \cdot FF_i \cdot Q_i \cdot S_{wet,i} \right) + D_{misc} / q
children.label: eq:drag-buildup
children.html: (CD,0)⋅Sref=∑i(Cf,i⋅FFi⋅Qi⋅Swet,i)+Dmisc/q(C_{D,0}) \cdot S_{ref} = \sum_{i} \left( C_{f,i} \cdot FF_i \cdot Q_i \cdot S_{wet,i} \right) + D_{misc} / q(CD,0​)⋅Sref​=i∑​(Cf,i​⋅FFi​⋅Qi​⋅Swet,i​)+Dmisc​/q

children.value: For conceptual design, assuming a 100% turbulent boundary layer provides a simpler, albeit slightly conservative, estimate. We use the Schlichting formula:

children.label: eq:skin-friction
children.html: Cf=0.455(log⁡10Re)2.58C_f = \frac{0.455}{(\log_{10} Re)^{2.58}}Cf​=(log10​Re)2.580.455​
Re=ρVlμRe = \frac{\rho V l}{\mu}Re=μρVl​

children.value: is calculated using the characteristic length

children.html: lll

children.value: 3D-printed surfaces typically have higher surface roughness due to layer lines. Unless extensively sanded and smoothed, assuming fully turbulent flow is both realistic and safe for our UAV designs.

children.html: SwetS_{wet}Swet​

children.value: The wetted area is the total surface exposed to the flow.
Can be approximated as cylinders with rounded or open ends.

children.html: Swet,f≈πDflf(1−2λf+λf232)S_{wet,f} \approx \pi D_f l_f \left( 1 - \frac{2}{\lambda_f} + \frac{\sqrt[3]{\lambda_f^2}}{2} \right)Swet,f​≈πDf​lf​⎝⎛​1−λf​2​+23λf2​​​⎠⎞​

children.value: Approximately twice the planform area plus a small correction for thickness.

Q≈1.5Q \approx 1.5Q≈1.5

Sometimes, drag is reported as an “equivalent flat plate area” or “drag area” (

children.html: fef_efe​

children.value: ). This is simply the parasite drag coefficient multiplied by the reference area:

children.label: eq:flat-plate-area
children.html: fe=CD,0⋅Sreff_e = C_{D,0} \cdot S_{ref}fe​=CD,0​⋅Sref​

children.value: This is very useful for comparing the “cleanliness” of different aircraft sizes or for handling “unusual” components like landing gears or struts.
Typical Component Flat Plate Areas (from Raymer)

ΔCD,0=feSref\Delta C_{D,0} = \frac{f_e}{S_{ref}}ΔCD,0​=Sref​fe​​

children.value: Even after a careful drag build-up, practical aircraft have “dirty” features that add drag.
Gaps in control surfaces, cooling vents, antennas. For small propeller aircraft (like our UAVs), this typically adds 5% to 10% to the total

children.html: CD,0C_{D,0}CD,0​

children.html: >15∘> 15^\circ>15∘
